coredata.dc:title: Binding effect of tyrosine-based chiral carbon dots on human serum albumin and lysozyme
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Hallaji, Zahra
Bagheri, Zeinab
Tavassoli, Zeinab
Ranjbar, Bijan
coredata.dc:description: Recently, chiral carbon dots (CCDs) have generated significant interest in biological fields such as biosensing, bioimaging, and drug delivery, due to the combination of inherent chirality and nanoscale size. Here, spectroscopic methods have been used to monitor the interaction between human serum albumin (HSA) and lysozyme (LYZ) with CCDs. Long-wavelength emission CCDs (LWE-CCDs) were synthesized using L/D-tyrosine (L/D-Tyr) and o-phenylenediamine (o-PDA) through a facile and one-step solvothermal approach. Two mentioned proteins interacted with L- and D-CDs and the results of secondary structure studies revealed that proteins preserve their native structure while having a small increase in their compactness. Furthermore, studies on the tertiary structure indicated more flexibility and the possibility of disorder in the hydrophobic regions of HSA, while LYZ appeared to become more rigid. Generally, the L- and D-CDs have more effect on the secondary and tertiary structures of HSA and LYZ, respectively. In addition, the interaction of L/D-Tyr (chiral precursor) with proteins was studied using molecular docking to explain the different effects of the CCDs on HSA and LYZ, and the obtained results are almost consistent with the results of experimental tests. This research offers new perspectives on the synthesis of LWE-CCD and the impact of their chirality on protein-nanoparticle interactions. Studying the action of CCDs at the molecular level will help researchers to choose suitable isomers to apply in biological applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chiral carbon dots
Protein
Interaction
Structure
Molecular docking

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Interaction of CCDs with HSA and LYZ
Interaction of CCDs with HSA and LYZ

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge financial support provided by Tarbiat Modares University and Shahid Beheshti University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV-visible spectrum of L-CDs, (b) Excitation wavelength-dependent emission spectra of l-CDs (the inset image shows L-CDs solution under 365 nm excitation), and (c, d) Circular dichroism spectra of L/D-Tyr and L/D-CDs, respectively.
FT-IR spectra of L- and D-CDs.
(a) TEM image, and (b) Raman spectrum of l-CDs.
UV-visible spectra of HSA (a, b), and LYZ (c, d) at various concentrations of L- and D-CDs (µg.mL−1), respectively. L-60 i.e. L-CDs with a concentration of 60 µg.mL−1 in the solution of protein.

Far-UV circular dichroism spectra of HSA (0.2 mg.mL−1) (a, b), and LYZ (0.2 mg.mL−1) (c, d) at various concentrations of L- and D-CDs (µg.mL−1), respectively. L-60 i.e. L-CDs with a concentration of 60 µg.mL−1 in the solution of protein.
(a) FT-IR spectra of HSA and HSA/L- or d-CDs complex, and (b) FT-IR spectra of LYZ and LYZ/L- or d-CDs complex. The concentration of HSA or LYZ is 0.2 mg.mL−1, and l- or d-CDs is 60 µg.mL−1.

Near-UV circular dichroism spectra of HSA (0.2 mg.mL−1) (a, b), and LYZ (0.2 mg.mL−1) (c, d) at various concentrations of L- and D-CDs (µg.mL−1), respectively. L-60 i.e. L-CDs with a concentration of 60 µg.mL−1 in the solution of protein.

Intrinsic fluorescence spectra (λex= 295 nm) of HSA (a, b), and LYZ (c, d) at various concentrations of L- and D-CDs (µg.mL−1), respectively. l-60 i.e. l-CDs with a concentration of 60 µg.mL−1 in the solution of protein.

ANS fluorescence spectra (λex= 350 nm) in the presence of HSA (a, b), and LYZ (c, d) at various concentrations of L- and D-CDs (µg.mL−1), respectively. L-60 i.e. L-CDs with a concentration of 60 µg.mL−1 in the solution of ANS/protein. The inset spectra in c and d show spectra at 415-650 nm.
Fluorescence spectra (λex= 350 nm) of ANS, L- and D-CDs, and ANS/L- or D-CDs. L-60 i.e. L-CDs with a concentration of 60 µg.mL−1. The concentration of ANS was 0.03 mg.mL−1.
HADDOCK (left) and MOE (right) results for the interaction of L/D-Tyr with HSA (a, b), and LYZ (c, d).
Estimation of the α-helical content of HSA and LYZ with increasing concentration of CCDs.
HADDOCK results for the interaction of L/D-Tyr with HSA and LYZ.

head.ce:title: Binding effect of tyrosine-based chiral carbon dots on human serum albumin and lysozyme

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanobiotechnology, Faculty of Biological Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Nanobiotechnology, Faculty of Biological Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Cell & Molecular Biology, Faculty of Life Sciences and Biotechnology, Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Sciences and Biotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Cell & Molecular Biology, Faculty of Life Sciences and Biotechnology, Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biology, Islamic Azad University Central Tehran Branch, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Islamic Azad University Central Tehran Branch

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cDepartment of Biology, Islamic Azad University Central Tehran Branch, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biophysics, Faculty of Biological Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: dDepartment of Biophysics, Faculty of Biological Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, chiral carbon dots (CCDs) have generated significant interest in biological fields such as biosensing, bioimaging, and drug delivery, due to the combination of inherent chirality and nanoscale size. Here, spectroscopic methods have been used to monitor the interaction between human serum albumin (HSA) and lysozyme (LYZ) with CCDs. Long-wavelength emission CCDs (LWE-CCDs) were synthesized using L/D-tyrosine (L/D-Tyr) and o-phenylenediamine (o-PDA) through a facile and one-step solvothermal approach. Two mentioned proteins interacted with L- and D-CDs and the results of secondary structure studies revealed that proteins preserve their native structure while having a small increase in their compactness. Furthermore, studies on the tertiary structure indicated more flexibility and the possibility of disorder in the hydrophobic regions of HSA, while LYZ appeared to become more rigid. Generally, the L- and D-CDs have more effect on the secondary and tertiary structures of HSA and LYZ, respectively. In addition, the interaction of L/D-Tyr (chiral precursor) with proteins was studied using molecular docking to explain the different effects of the CCDs on HSA and LYZ, and the obtained results are almost consistent with the results of experimental tests. This research offers new perspectives on the synthesis of LWE-CCD and the impact of their chirality on protein-nanoparticle interactions. Studying the action of CCDs at the molecular level will help researchers to choose suitable isomers to apply in biological applications.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chirality is a common and intriguing natural occurrence, evident in protein and DNA, as well as in supramolecular and nanoscopic structures. It is crucial in a variety of fields such as chemistry, biology, pharmacology, and medicine [1]. The molecular isomers with different chirality may have noticeably distinct biological effects despite sharing comparable chemical structures.

Carbon dots (CDs) are novel carbon-containing fluorescent nanoparticles with a size below 10 nm [2-4] that have demonstrated promising biological applications such as bioimaging and drug delivery [5-7]. If chirality were introduced, the use of CDs in biological and medical areas would be considerably enhanced [8,9]. The synthesis of chiral carbon dots (CCDs) can be categorized into three approaches: one-step, two-step, and co/self-assembly. The two-step method is complex, involving the initial creation of non-chiral CDs followed by the attachment of chiral molecules. The co/self-assembly approach depends on the inherent assembly properties of molecules for chirality but requires stringent conditions due to environmental sensitivity. The one-step method is simpler, using a carbon source and chiral ligand in a system, but demands precise control over reaction parameters to ensure chirality inheritance [9], particularly for creating CCDs with long-wavelength emission (LWE-CCD). The use of precursors with aromatic structure, high reaction temperature, acidic solvents, and solvents with low polarity helps in the synthesis of LWE-CCDs. However, achieving LWE-CCD synthesis is challenging due to the non-conjugated nature and water solubility of most chiral precursors (amino acids) and the risk of excessive carbonization at high temperatures, which can destroy the chiral structure of chiral precursors [10]. Therefore, developing a straightforward method for preparing LWE-CCDs is challenging.

CCDs have recently been shown to offer appealing potential for biological applications [11-13], especially LWE-CCDs in bioimaging for preventing reabsorption and overlapping with autofluorescence of tissues [14]. Therefore, research into how CCDs affect proteins or cells in biological systems is essential. Based on our studies, there are several reports on the toxicity of CCDs to cells [11,13,15,16], but just limited studies on how CCDs affect proteins [17-19]. Fluorescent CDs produced using various synthetic processes and precursors have diverse optical properties, in addition to varying particle sizes and surface chemical characteristics, which means that each type of CD will have a unique impact on the structure and function of proteins. The pharmacokinetic behavior of CCDs can be better understood, and their molecular and biological impacts on proteins can be clarified by studying how CCDs interact with proteins such as human serum albumin (HSA) or lysozyme (LYZ) [20]. A highly abundant protein in blood plasma is HSA (66.5 kDa), which has some crucial biological tasks such as preserving the osmotic plasma pressure and transport of various substances [21,22]. LYZ is a monomeric, small (14.7 kDa), and globular protein. It is crucial in the defense against gram-positive bacterial infections and can damage bacterial cell wall structures [23]. Both proteins are extensively employed in interaction studies between proteins and nanoparticles [24-28]. However, very few studies have looked at how CCDs interact with these proteins. As a result, this work has concentrated on how CCDs interact with HSA and LYZ as model proteins.

Here, L/D-tyrosine (L/D-Tyr) and o-phenylenediamine (o-PDA) were used as starting materials for a facile and one-step solvothermal technique to synthesize the LWE-CCDs. Additionally, the morphology, diameter distribution, structure, and optical properties of CCDs were studied using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and different spectroscopy techniques. After that, UV–visible absorption, circular dichroism spectropolarimetry, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), and fluorescence spectroscopy were utilized to explore the interaction between the CCDs with HSA and LYZ. In addition, molecular docking was used to investigate the interaction between L/D-Tyr (chiral precursor) with HSA and LYZ. According to our studies, the effect of the chirality of CDs in the interaction with HSA and LYZ has not yet been reported. These methods may help predict how CCDs will behave at the nano-bio interface and greatly facilitate the suitable use of CCDs in biomedical applications.

HSA, sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), sodium phosphate dibasic (Na2HPO4), potassium phosphate monobasic (KH2PO4), L/D-Tyr, o-PDA, 8-anilino naphthalene-1-sulfonic acid (ANS), and sulfuric acid (H2SO4) were purchased from Merck. LYZ was purchased from Bio Basic. Cellulose dialysis membrane (3500 Da) was acquired from Thermo Fisher Scientific. Deionized water was used in all experiments.

Using a Cary 100 UV-visible spectrophotometer, UV-visible absorption spectra were measured. The Perkin-Elmer LS-45 luminescence spectrometer was used to measure fluorescence spectra. J-715 circular dichroism spectropolarimeter was used to record circular dichroism spectra. On the Nicolet IR 100 spectrometer, FT-IR spectra between 400 and 4000 cm−1 were captured. Zeiss-EM10C microscope captured TEM images. A few droplets of well-sonicated CCD solution were applied to the carbon/Formvar film that was mounted on an EMS-USA 300-mesh copper grid before imaging, and dried at room temperature. The Metrohm 827 pH meter was used to measure each pH value.

Briefly, 0.02 g of L-Tyr was dissolved in 9.75 mL of deionized water. After that, 250 µL of H2SO4 was added drop by drop to the solution. The obtained solution was placed on the heater stirrer at a temperature of 45 °C, and 0.01 g of o-PDA was added to it. Finally, the transparent solution was transferred onto the 25 mL stainless steel autoclave, and then heated at 160 °C for 7 h in the oven. The obtained solution was dark green, which confirmed the preliminary preparation of L-CDs. For 30 min, this solution was centrifuged at 13,200 relative centrifugal force (RCF). The sediment was discarded, and the supernatant was collected. The solution was filtered with a syringe filter (0.22 µm) to get rid of extra precursors and other impurities, and then further purified by a dialysis membrane with a 3500 cut-off until the pH of the solution reached 7. In this case, the color of the solution is almost pale orange. After this, it is kept at 4 °C. D-CDs were also synthesized by the same method, with the difference that D-Tyr was used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of CCDs with HSA and LYZ
ce:section.ce:para: A certain concentration of the proteins (stock concentration: 1 mg.mL−1) was treated with 0, 1.8, 3.7, 7.5, 15, 30, and 60 µg.mL−1 of purified L- and D-CDs in the presence of phosphate buffer saline (PBS, 1X, pH 7.4). Since a longer incubation period had no discernible impact on HSA and LYZ interactions with L- and D-CDs, these samples were incubated at 4 °C for 1 h. UV-visible, circular dichroism and fluorescence spectroscopy have all been conducted using this approach. The UV–visible absorption spectra were measured, and the path length was fixed at 1 cm. The final concentrations of HSA and LYZ were kept at 0.7 and 0.2 mg.mL−1, respectively. The far- and near-UV circular dichroism spectra were scanned in the range of 200–250 nm (path length of cell: 0.1 cm) and 250-320 nm (path length of cell: 1 cm), respectively. The concentrations of HSA and LYZ were kept at 0.2 mg.mL−1 for these measurements. The results of the analysis were presented as molar ellipticity [θ] based on the mean amino acid residue weight (MRW) that [θ]=θ × 100 MRW/cl. Where θ is the measured ellipticity in degree at wavelength λ, l is the length of the light path in cm, and c is the protein concentration in mg.mL−1.

In addition, the intrinsic and extrinsic (ANS) fluorescence emission spectrum was scanned with 295 and 350 nm as the excitation wavelength, respectively. In order to record the spectra, the path length was fixed at 1 cm. The final concentrations of HSA and LYZ were kept at 0.5 and 0.1 mg.mL−1 for intrinsic fluorescence studies, and 0.05 and 0.1 mg.mL−1 for extrinsic fluorescence studies, respectively. ANS concentrations were 0.03 and 0.5 mg.mL−1 for HSA and LYZ, respectively. Moreover, FT-IR spectra of HSA, LYZ, and HSA/LYZ–CDs (L and D) systems were recorded in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 after lyophilization. The concentration of HSA and LYZ was 0.2 mg.mL−1. The concentrations of L- and D-CDs were 60 µg.mL−1.

The three-dimensional structure of HSA (protein data bank (PDB) ID: 1AO6) and hen egg white LYZ (PDB ID: 2YVB) were obtained from the RCSB PDB. The structure of L/D-Tyr was acquired from PubChem as SDF files. The SDF files were converted to MOL2 format using Open Babel. Since it is not possible to determine the exact structure of CDs, it was decided to use the structure of L/D-Tyr for these studies, which causes chirality in the structure of L- and D-CDs. The process of molecular docking was conducted using the HADDOCK web server [29]. HADDOCK was chosen for its unique capability to incorporate user-defined restraints, which guide the docking simulations toward specific binding sites. Unlike traditional grid-based methods, HADDOCK allows for a more tailored and flexible docking process. The restraints used in HADDOCK are based on prior knowledge, such as known interacting residues or functional groups. To fully leverage the strengths of the HADDOCK, it was employed to identify potential binding sites with greater precision. Specifically, residues that had been previously reported to be associated with the binding sites of the HSA and LYZ were incorporated into the input files of the HADDOCK. The residues glutamic acid-35 (Glu-35) and aspartic acid-52 (Asp-52) have been identified as critical binding sites for the LYZ. These specific amino acids play a crucial role in the enzyme's interaction and functionality [30,31]. Additionally, the main binding site for HSA referred to as IIA (Sudlow site 1) and IIIA (Sudlow site 2) [32,33] was specified in the input configuration file of the HADDOCK. The PDB files of HSA and LYZ served as the target structures, while the MOL2 files of L/D-Tyr were uploaded for analysis. Ligand interactions were obtained from the Molecular Operating Environment (MOE) [34].

L- and D-CDs were successfully prepared by a solvothermal method using L/D-Tyr and o-PDA as precursors. It should be mentioned that because L/D-Tyr have the same chemical and physical properties and only have different optical activities, most of the characterization has been done on L-CDs.

Fig. 1
a shows the UV-visible spectrum of L-CDs. The spectrum shows two peaks at ∼230 and ∼280 nm that are related to the π-π* and n-π* transitions of the aromatic C = C bonds and C = N/C = O bonds in sp2 systems, respectively. In addition, the l-CDs exhibited wide absorption at 320-550 nm may be due to the other functionalized surface groups of CDs [35,36]. In Fig. 1 b, the L-CDs' photoluminescence (PL) is displayed. L-CDs solution showed orange PL when excited at 365 nm (Fig. 1 b inset). It should be noted that when the excitation wavelength was altered from 360 to 560 nm, the maximum emission wavelength of L-CDs shifted from 573 to 611 nm, demonstrating that l-CDs display excitation-dependent PL. Circular dichroism spectropolarimetry was used to investigate the chiral characteristics of the produced CDs. While the circular dichroism spectra of L/D-Tyr exhibit a characteristic peak around 278 nm (Fig. 1 c), the l- and d-CDs show two peaks at 260–280 nm (Fig. 1 d), demonstrating the Tyr structural changes during synthesis and contribution in the CDs structure. The inherent chirality of Tyr is the cause of the CD's peak around 278 nm. Moreover, the additional signal in the CD spectra is formed in a chiral environment via the π-π* and n-π* transitions [10].

Based on the FT-IR spectra of L- and D-CDs in Fig. 2 , the stretching vibrations of -OH and NH are thought to be responsible for the absorption bands at 3200–3400 cm−1 [37,38]. At 2959 cm−1, CH bond vibrations were also noted [39]. The bending vibration of CO and CO-C is said to be responsible for the peaks at 1107 cm−1 [40,41]. The CN bond is attributed to the peak at 2361 cm−1 [42]. The peaks at 1574 and 1503 cm−1 correlated with the stretching vibration of C = N and C = C [43]. The stretching of -CONH is shown by the band at 1328 cm−1 [10].

In Fig. 3
a, the l-CDs' TEM image can be seen. The image can be used to identify the spherical forms of l-CDs, which have an average particle size of 5.1 ± 1.2 nm. In addition, Raman spectroscopy is used to investigate the degree of graphitization that occurs during the synthesis of CDs (Fig. 3 b). The G and D bands show the sp2 carbon network and defects in the CD structure, which appeared at 1581 and 1371 cm−1, respectively. A higher degree of graphitization in the CD structure is indicated by a lower ID/IG number ratio. Our synthetic L-CDs have a measured ID/IG ratio of 0.90, which indicates that there are roughly equal amounts of defects and graphitized structures. A significant share of defects is caused by the presence of surface functional groups [44,45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of CCDs with HSA and LYZ

ce:section.ce:para:
UV–visible absorption spectroscopy is a powerful technique for characterizing protein structure change. Variations in the absorption spectrum within the far- and near-UV regions suggest modifications in the protein's secondary and tertiary structures, respectively [46]. The absorption of proteins in the range of 180-230 nm mainly represents π-π* transitions of the peptide bond but absorption in the range of 230-300 nm is dominated by the aromatic amino acids [47]. Herein, the UV–visible absorption spectra of HSA and LYZ in different concentrations of L- and D-CDs were recorded (Figs. 4
a-d). The absorbance peaks of two proteins increased gradually with the increase of L- and D-CD concentrations. Meanwhile, the position of the first peak showed a slight redshift, but the second peak remained unaltered. Consequently, spectral changes at the far- and near-UV regions signify that the CCDs affect secondary and tertiary structures of proteins due to the interactions between L- and D-CDs with HSA and LYZ.

Circular dichroism is an excellent technique for studying the structural changes of biological macromolecules because the interaction of a chiral molecule with a polarized light is very particular [48,49]. To determine the protein's secondary structure, circular dichroism spectropolarimetry in the 200-250 nm region (far-UV) can be utilized, in which the peptide bond serves as the chromophore at these wavelengths [50]. The variations in the circular dichroism spectra of HSA and LYZ in the absence and presence of L- and D-CDs are shown in Figs. 5
a–d, and CCDs cause the change of proteins' secondary structure upon binding. Native LYZ and HSA show two minima at 208 and 222 nm, which are the characteristics of a typical α-helical dominant protein [51]. In the presence of L- and D-CDs, the characteristic bands at 208 and 222 nm exhibit greater negative values, indicating an increase in the compactness of HSA and LYZ and is not highly concentration dependent. However, the circular dichroism data in Figs. 5 a and d indicate that l- and d-CDs had a more significant effect on HSA and LYZ structure, respectively.

In order to quantify the value of the typical a-helical content of HSA and LYZ, circular dichroism spectra can be analyzed through the following equation, α-Helix (%)= -([θ]222+2340)/30,300 × 100. Herein, [θ]222 is the molar ellipticity at 222 nm [52,53]. The α-helical content of model proteins i.e. HSA and LYZ, in the absence and presence of CCDs, has been calculated and summarized in Table 1 . It was found that the α-helical content increased in HSA and LYZ in the presence of CCDs. As can be seen, L- and D-CDs have more effects on the α-helical content of HSA and LYZ, respectively.

Another useful technique for keeping track of modifications to proteins' secondary structures and interactions with other molecules is FT-IR spectroscopy [54]. For the complex of proteins/CCDs, FT-IR spectra can show interactions between them as well as changes in the conformation of the secondary structures of HSA and LYZ. The bands resulting from amide I and amide II vibrations in FT-IR spectra (Figs. 6
a and b) are sensitive to changes in the secondary structure of proteins. The amide II peak (primarily CN stretch coupled with NH bending mode) can be found in the range of 1500–1550 cm−1, while the amide I peak appears in the range of 1600–1700 cm−1 (mostly C = O stretch) [55]. Upon CCDs addition, the C = O stretching, NH bending, and CN stretching vibration of proteins decreased in the presence of CDs, demonstrating these groups of HSA and LYZ were involved in the interaction and conformational change that occurred in HSA and LYZ [56]. Notably, these effects are greater with the L-CDs on HSA and the D-CDs on LYZ. The results identified by the FT-IR are in good agreement with the circular dichroism data.

A protein's circular dichroism spectrum can reveal specific details of its tertiary structure by aromatic amino acids in the 250–320 nm region (near-UV) [50]. The near-UV circular dichroism signals of HSA in the presence of L- and D-CDs (Figs. 7
a and b) are lower than free HSA, suggesting the addition of L- and D-CDs will induce a looser and more flexible tertiary structure [48,57]. Notably, L-CD is slightly more effective on the tertiary structure of HSA. In addition, the near-UV circular dichroism spectrum of LYZ (Figs. 7 c and d) in the presence of L- and D-CDs displays an increase, which is associated with the indole group of tryptophan (Trp) residues. Consequently, the rigidity of LYZ increases and D-CD is slightly more effective on the tertiary structure of it. Finally, the interaction of L- and D-CDs with HSA and LYZ partly affects their tertiary structure.

To understand the effect of l- and d-CDs on the third structure of HSA and LYZ, an intrinsic fluorescence method was also employed. External effects may affect the position of Trp residues in hydrophilic or hydrophobic regions and cause a change in the intrinsic fluorescence intensity of the protein. The presence of Trp residues in the hydrophobic and hydrophilic regions is indicated by an increase and a decrease in intrinsic fluorescence intensity, respectively. So, if the intensity decreases, the local rigidity of the structure is reduced, and if it increases, the local rigidity increases [50,58].

The intrinsic fluorescence of HSA and LYZ is predominantly provided by Trp residues with excitation at 295 nm [20]. Figs. 8
a and b illustrate the gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity and slight redshift in the maximum emission wavelength of HSA, along with an increase in the concentration of L- and D-CDs. This effect suggests that CCDs interacted with HSA and could efficiently reduce the local rigidity of structure, which may be due to the complex formation between the protein and CCDs. In other words, CCDs may affect the hydrophobicity of the Trp environment due to the presence of hydrophilic groups on CCDs, giving a more flexible structure around this residue. These results are consistent with the results of the near-UV circular dichroism spectropolarimetry but in this effect, there is no significant difference between the effects of the L and D isomers of CDs on HSA. However, LYZ fluorescence intensity was increased in the presence of L- and D-CDs and showed a little redshift (Figs. 8 c and d). These outcomes are consistent with the results of the near-UV circular dichroism spectropolarimetry i.e. increase in rigidity. LYZ contains 6 Trp residues (28, 62, 63, 108, 111, and 123) that Trp-62, Trp-63, and Trp-108 situated in the active site but HSA has only one Trp residue. The majority of the LYZ emission is due to the Trp-62 and Trp-108 [59], hence the intensity change is most likely owing to those Trp residues that were previously quenched but might be excited in the presence of CCDs. In this case, d-CD is slightly more effective.

To verify the outcomes of intrinsic fluorescence and identify any quenching, researchers conduct extrinsic fluorescence experiments using ANS. The characteristic of ANS is an increase in its emission intensity upon binding to the hydrophobic regions of proteins [50]. As depicted in Figs. 9
a-d, with the increase in CCD concentrations, there was a reduction in the fluorescence intensity of ANS across all samples.

In the case of HSA, according to the previous results (Figs. 7 (a, b) and 8 (a, b)), the presence of CCDs increases the flexibility of the tertiary structure, which means that the hydrophobic regions of the protein become more accessible, and therefore ANS should be able to bind to these regions and increase its fluorescence emission. However, according to Figs. 9 a and b, ANS's fluorescence emission is reduced, which strengthens the function of CCD as a quenching agent. To ensure this assumption, the excitation and emission of L- and D-CDs in the area belonging to ANS were investigated, and using the highest concentration of L- and D-CDs in interaction studies (60 µg.mL−1), they showed a very low intensity of emission. However, in the presence of ANS, they did not show the ability to reduce its emission (Fig. 10 ), i.e. they cannot act as a quenching agent for ANS. Therefore, these data and circular dichroism data strengthen the increase in flexibility and disorder in the hydrophobic regions of HSA, which reduces the binding ability of ANS to its hydrophobic regions.

Also, the data regarding LYZ (Figs. 9 c and d) suggest that the protein exhibits fewer accessible hydrophobic regions when CCDs are present, leading to a reduced ability of ANS to attach to the protein's hydrophobic regions and a decrease in its fluorescence. These findings reveal an increase in protein rigidity with the addition of CCDs that are aligned with the data obtained from near-UV circular dichroism and intrinsic fluorescence studies (Figs. 7 (c, d) and 8 (c, d)). In addition, in these studies, the effect of the L-CDs on HSA and D-CDs on LYZ is greater.

The binding of L- and D-Tyr to the main binding sites of HSA (IIA: 197–297 and IIIA: 384–497) and LYZ (Glu-35 and Asp-52) was investigated using the HADDOCK web server and MOE. The results are shown in Fig. 11 and Table 2 . As previously mentioned, the molecular and three-dimensional structures of the L- and D-CDs are not known. Therefore, these chirality-inducing agents serve as ligands.

Based on the docking analysis (Table 2), the binding affinities of different complexes involving HSA and LYZ with L/D-Tyr were identified. About LYZ, d-Tyr exhibited a more negative Z-score (−2), indicating the more strong binding affinity toward LYZ in comparison to l-Tyr (−1.7). These results are in line with the results of experimental parts that d-CDs cause more structural changes in LYZ. In the case of HSA, L/D-Tyr shows a similar binding affinity but according to the experimental results, l-CDs have exerted a greater effect on the structure of HSA. The Van der Waals and electrostatic energy signify the strength of Van der Waals and electrostatic interactions, which based on the docking analysis, play an effective role in the interaction between L/D-Tyr with both proteins. Based on Table 2, it can be stated that the different effects of CD isomers on the structure of HSA and LYZ are mostly dependent on electrostatic and Van der Waals interactions, respectively. Finally, lower desolvation energy typically means that the binding complex is more stable in a solvated environment, contributing positively to the overall binding affinity. It is observed that HSA complexes exhibit more negative desolvation energy than LYZ, which generally implies a more favorable binding due to better solvation interactions. In this case, there is not much difference between L and D isomers, especially in LYZ. These insights provide a comprehensive understanding of the molecular interactions and binding stability of the evaluated complexes.

Here, fluorescent CCDs have been produced by a solvothermal method using L/D-Tyr and o-PDA. The different properties of the CCDs were characterized by TEM, FT-IR, Raman, fluorescence, circular dichroism, and UV-visible spectroscopy. Several spectroscopic techniques, including UV-visible, FT-IR, circular dichroism, and fluorescence were used to explore the effect of L- and D-CDs on the secondary and tertiary structure of HSA and LYZ. Secondary structure studies showed that L- and D-CDs could increase the compactness of two proteins. In addition, tertiary structure studies revealed more flexibility and the possibility of disorder in the hydrophobic regions of HSA and more rigidity in LYZ. The important point in all results is the greater effect of l-CD on HSA and D-CD on LYZ that the results of molecular docking with L/D-Tyr (chirality-inducing agent) showed almost this phenomenon. In summary, chirality in nanomaterials is a fundamental property that can significantly influence their physical, chemical, and biological behaviors. Therefore, the chirality of nanomaterials is a key factor in their interactions with proteins, affecting everything from drug delivery to disease treatment. Previous reports also showed that the existence of chirality is effective in the biological applications of CCDs [60]. So, in this work, due to the difference in the effect of synthesized L- and D-CDs in the interaction with proteins, these isomers may also have different effects with better performance of an isomer in biological applications such as bioimaging, biosensing, regulation of enzyme activity, anti-bacterial/anti-biofilm activity, and anti-cancer/anti-disease activity. However, this assumption needs in vitro or in vivo experiments and should be investigated in future studies.

Zahra Hallaji: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Investigation. Zeinab Bagheri: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Methodology. Zeinab Tavassoli: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Bijan Ranjbar: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial support provided by Tarbiat Modares University and Shahid Beheshti University.
